title: Auto-configuration
question: What is Spring Boot auto-configuration?
answer: Spring Boot scans the classpath and applies sensible default configurations (data sources, MVC, security) so you don't wire them by hand.
explanation: You can override or exclude specific auto-configurations.
codeExample:
@SpringBootApplication
public class App { public static void main(String[] a) { SpringApplication.run(App.class, a); } }
bestPractices: Prefer starters (spring-boot-starter-*).
commonMistakes: Conflicting beans across custom configs.
interviewTips: Explain @EnableAutoConfiguration behind @SpringBootApplication.

title: Dependency Injection
question: How does Spring DI work?
answer: The IoC container creates and wires beans annotated with @Component/@Service/@Repository/@Configuration; you inject via constructor, setter, or field.
explanation: Constructor injection is preferred for immutability and testability.
codeExample:
@Service
class Users { private final Repo repo; Users(Repo repo) { this.repo = repo; } }
bestPractices: Prefer constructor injection.
commonMistakes: Circular dependencies via field injection.
interviewTips: Show @Qualifier for disambiguation.

title: Spring Data JPA
question: What does Spring Data JPA give you?
answer: Repository interfaces (extending JpaRepository) with generated CRUD, paging, and derived queries by method name.
explanation: Use @Query for custom JPQL/native queries.
bestPractices: Avoid N+1 queries via fetch joins.
commonMistakes: Lazy loading outside a transaction (LazyInitializationException).
interviewTips: Explain @Transactional propagation.

title: REST Controllers
question: How do you build REST APIs with Spring MVC?
answer: Annotate a class with @RestController and methods with @GetMapping/@PostMapping etc. Return objects and Spring serializes to JSON via Jackson.
explanation: Combine with @Valid and @RequestBody for validation.
bestPractices: Return proper HTTP status codes.
commonMistakes: Leaking entities directly in API responses.
interviewTips: Mention @ControllerAdvice for global exceptions.

title: Profiles & Config
question: How do Spring profiles work?
answer: Profiles (dev, prod, test) activate different beans and property files (application-dev.yml). Set via spring.profiles.active.
explanation: Useful for environment-specific config without code changes.
codeExample: @Profile("dev") @Bean DataSource devDs() { /* H2 */ }
bestPractices: Externalise secrets — don't commit them.
commonMistakes: Multiple beans active in overlapping profiles.
interviewTips: Discuss Spring Cloud Config for centralised config.

title: Bean Scopes & Lifecycle
question: What bean scopes does Spring support and how is a bean's lifecycle managed?
answer: Common scopes are singleton (default, one per container), prototype (new instance per lookup), and web scopes request/session/application. The lifecycle runs: instantiation, dependency injection, @PostConstruct, in-use, then @PreDestroy on shutdown.
explanation: Singletons are eagerly created and cached; prototypes are created on each request and Spring does not manage their destruction. Lifecycle callbacks let you initialise or release resources cleanly.
bestPractices: Keep singleton beans stateless and thread-safe.
Use @PostConstruct for initialisation that needs injected dependencies.
commonMistakes: Injecting a prototype into a singleton and expecting a fresh instance each call.
Relying on @PreDestroy for prototype beans (it is not invoked).
interviewTips: Explain why singleton beans must be stateless.
Mention ObjectFactory/Provider for prototype-in-singleton problems.

title: @Component vs @Bean
question: What is the difference between @Component and @Bean?
answer: @Component (and stereotypes @Service/@Repository/@Controller) marks a class for classpath scanning so Spring auto-detects and registers it. @Bean is a method-level annotation inside a @Configuration class where you explicitly construct and return the bean.
explanation: Use @Component for your own classes you control; use @Bean when you need to configure third-party classes you cannot annotate, or need programmatic control over construction.
bestPractices: Use @Bean for third-party library objects.
Keep @Configuration classes focused and cohesive.
commonMistakes: Annotating library classes you cannot modify with @Component.
Forgetting component scanning does not reach classes outside the base package.
interviewTips: Emphasise 'ownership': your class vs external class.
Note both end up as beans in the same container.

title: application.properties vs YAML
question: How do you externalise configuration with application.properties or application.yml?
answer: Spring Boot reads key/value settings from application.properties or the hierarchical application.yml on the classpath. Values are bound to beans and accessible via @Value or @ConfigurationProperties, and can be overridden by environment variables or command-line args.
explanation: YAML is more readable for nested structures and lists; properties is flat. Property source ordering means later sources (env vars, CLI) override earlier ones.
bestPractices: Prefer @ConfigurationProperties over many @Value fields.
Keep secrets out of committed config; use env vars or a vault.
commonMistakes: Mixing tabs into YAML (only spaces are allowed).
Assuming properties override YAML when both exist without knowing precedence.
interviewTips: Explain property source ordering and override precedence.
Mention profile-specific files like application-prod.yml.

title: Spring Boot Starters
question: What are Spring Boot starters?
answer: Starters are curated dependency descriptors (e.g. spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa) that pull in a coherent set of libraries with compatible versions, so you add one dependency instead of many.
explanation: They work together with the Spring Boot BOM/parent, which manages versions so you rarely specify version numbers yourself.
codeExample:
<!-- pom.xml -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-validation</artifactId>
</dependency>
bestPractices: Rely on the Boot parent/BOM for version management.
Only add the starters you actually use to keep the jar lean.
commonMistakes: Overriding managed versions and creating conflicts.
Adding both starter-web and starter-webflux without understanding the effect.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between a starter and the auto-configuration it triggers.
Mention spring-boot-starter-parent for dependency management.

title: Spring Security Basics
question: How does Spring Security secure an application?
answer: Spring Security inserts a servlet filter chain that authenticates requests and enforces authorisation rules. You configure a SecurityFilterChain bean defining which endpoints are public, which require roles, and how users authenticate.
explanation: By default it secures all endpoints with HTTP Basic and a generated password. In modern Boot you customise via the SecurityFilterChain lambda DSL rather than extending WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter.
bestPractices: Encode passwords with BCryptPasswordEncoder.
Deny by default and explicitly permit public routes.
commonMistakes: Disabling CSRF blindly for stateful browser sessions.
Storing plaintext passwords.
interviewTips: Differentiate authentication vs authorisation.
Explain the filter chain order and the SecurityContext.

title: JWT Authentication
question: How do you implement stateless JWT authentication in Spring Boot?
answer: On login you validate credentials and issue a signed JWT containing claims. Subsequent requests send the token in the Authorization header; a filter validates the signature and expiry, then populates the SecurityContext, so the server holds no session state.
explanation: JWTs enable horizontal scaling because any node can verify the token without shared session storage. Keep tokens short-lived and use refresh tokens for longer sessions.
bestPractices: Use short expiry plus refresh tokens.
Sign with a strong secret or asymmetric keys and store secrets securely.
commonMistakes: Putting sensitive data in the (base64, not encrypted) payload.
Not validating token expiration or signature server-side.
interviewTips: Contrast stateless JWT with server-side sessions.
Discuss token revocation strategies (blocklist, short TTL).

title: Global Exception Handling
question: How do you handle exceptions globally with @ControllerAdvice?
answer: A class annotated with @ControllerAdvice (or @RestControllerAdvice) contains @ExceptionHandler methods that catch exceptions thrown by any controller and translate them into consistent HTTP responses.
explanation: This centralises error mapping, keeps controllers clean, and lets you return a uniform error payload with proper status codes.
bestPractices: Return a consistent error DTO across the API.
Map domain exceptions to appropriate HTTP status codes.
commonMistakes: Catching Exception broadly and hiding the real cause.
Leaking stack traces or internal details to clients.
interviewTips: Mention ResponseEntityExceptionHandler for Spring MVC defaults.
Explain ordering when multiple handlers match.

title: Request Validation with @Valid
question: How do you validate incoming request bodies in Spring Boot?
answer: Annotate DTO fields with Bean Validation constraints (@NotNull, @Size, @Email) and add @Valid to the @RequestBody parameter. Spring validates before the handler runs and throws MethodArgumentNotValidException on failure.
explanation: Combined with @ControllerAdvice you can convert validation errors into a clean 400 response listing field errors. Requires the validation starter.
bestPractices: Validate at the boundary (controller) before business logic.
Return field-level error details to help API consumers.
commonMistakes: Forgetting spring-boot-starter-validation dependency.
Using @Validated vs @Valid without understanding group validation.
interviewTips: Explain how MethodArgumentNotValidException is handled globally.
Mention @Validated on the class for method-level validation.

title: @Transactional Propagation & Isolation
question: How do propagation and isolation work with @Transactional?
answer: @Transactional wraps a method in a database transaction. Propagation controls how it participates in existing transactions (REQUIRED joins, REQUIRES_NEW suspends and starts a new one). Isolation controls visibility of concurrent changes (READ_COMMITTED, REPEATABLE_READ, SERIALIZABLE).
explanation: Defaults are propagation REQUIRED and the database's default isolation. Choosing higher isolation reduces anomalies (dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads) at the cost of concurrency.
bestPractices: Keep transactions short to reduce lock contention.
Use REQUIRES_NEW for independent audit/logging writes.
commonMistakes: Calling a @Transactional method from within the same class (self-invocation bypasses the proxy).
Marking transactions readonly=false when only reading.
interviewTips: Explain the four isolation levels and the anomalies they prevent.
Note rollback only triggers on unchecked exceptions by default.

title: Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)
question: What is AOP in Spring and when would you use it?
answer: AOP lets you modularise cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions) into aspects that run at defined join points via advice (@Before, @After, @Around) matched by pointcut expressions, without cluttering business code.
explanation: Spring AOP uses proxies, so advice applies to Spring-managed beans on public method calls. @Transactional itself is implemented with AOP.
bestPractices: Keep pointcuts specific to avoid unintended matches.
Use AOP for infrastructure concerns, not core business logic.
commonMistakes: Expecting advice on self-invoked or private methods (proxy limitation).
Overly broad pointcuts that slow the whole app.
interviewTips: Explain proxy-based limitations vs AspectJ weaving.
Mention that @Transactional and @Cacheable are AOP-based.

title: Actuator & Metrics
question: What does Spring Boot Actuator provide?
answer: Actuator exposes production-ready endpoints (/actuator/health, /info, /metrics, /prometheus) for monitoring, health checks, and metrics via Micrometer, which integrates with Prometheus, Datadog, and others.
explanation: You control which endpoints are exposed over HTTP and secure them, since they can reveal sensitive operational data.
codeExample:
# application.yml
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,info,metrics,prometheus
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: when_authorized
bestPractices: Expose only needed endpoints and secure them.
Add custom health indicators for critical dependencies.
commonMistakes: Exposing all endpoints publicly, leaking env and config data.
Ignoring readiness/liveness probes in Kubernetes.
interviewTips: Explain Micrometer as the metrics facade.
Mention liveness vs readiness health groups.

title: Caching with @Cacheable
question: How does declarative caching with @Cacheable work?
answer: @EnableCaching activates Spring's cache abstraction. @Cacheable stores a method's result keyed by its arguments; on a cache hit the method body is skipped. @CacheEvict and @CachePut manage invalidation and updates.
explanation: Spring provides a pluggable abstraction backed by providers like Caffeine, Redis, or a simple ConcurrentHashMap. It is AOP-proxy based, so the same self-invocation caveat applies.
bestPractices: Set TTL and size limits to avoid stale or unbounded caches.
Evict or update the cache on writes to keep it consistent.
commonMistakes: Caching mutable objects that are later modified in place.
Forgetting @EnableCaching so annotations do nothing.
interviewTips: Explain cache key generation from method arguments.
Contrast @CachePut vs @Cacheable.

title: Scheduling with @Scheduled
question: How do you run scheduled tasks in Spring Boot?
answer: Enable scheduling with @EnableScheduling and annotate methods with @Scheduled using fixedRate, fixedDelay, or a cron expression. Spring runs them on a background scheduler thread pool.
explanation: fixedRate starts runs at a fixed interval regardless of duration; fixedDelay waits after the previous run finishes. Cron gives calendar-based control.
bestPractices: Configure a dedicated task scheduler pool for concurrent jobs.
Make jobs idempotent and guard against overlapping runs in clusters.
commonMistakes: Long-running jobs blocking the single default scheduler thread.
Running the same scheduled job on every node without coordination.
interviewTips: Explain fixedRate vs fixedDelay clearly.
Mention ShedLock or a leader election for clustered scheduling.

title: Asynchronous Execution with @Async
question: How does @Async enable asynchronous method execution?
answer: With @EnableAsync, methods annotated @Async run on a separate thread from a TaskExecutor, returning void or a Future/CompletableFuture. The caller does not block waiting for completion.
explanation: Like other proxy-based features, @Async only works on public methods called from another bean, and you should configure a bounded executor rather than the default.
bestPractices: Configure a bounded custom executor with a sensible queue.
Return CompletableFuture to compose and handle results/errors.
commonMistakes: Calling an @Async method from within the same class (proxy bypassed).
Relying on the unbounded default SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor.
interviewTips: Explain why self-invocation breaks @Async.
Discuss exception handling with AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler.

title: Testing with @SpringBootTest & MockMvc
question: How do you test Spring Boot applications with @SpringBootTest and MockMvc?
answer: @SpringBootTest loads the full application context for integration tests. MockMvc (via @AutoConfigureMockMvc or @WebMvcTest) lets you exercise controllers over a simulated HTTP layer without starting a real server.
explanation: @WebMvcTest is a slice test loading only the web layer for fast controller tests, while @SpringBootTest is heavier but exercises real wiring. Use @MockBean to stub collaborators.
bestPractices: Prefer slice tests (@WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest) for speed.
Use @MockBean to isolate the unit under test.
commonMistakes: Using @SpringBootTest for everything, slowing the suite.
Not resetting shared state between tests.
interviewTips: Contrast slice tests vs full-context tests.
Explain @MockBean vs plain Mockito mocks.
